section: Flood Control Dams in Beckham County
text:
Oklahoma has 2,107 flood control dams in 61 counties.
These dams have been constructed through local
watershed project sponsors with financial and technical
assistance from the USDA Natural Resources
Conservation Service (NRCS) authorized through Public
Law 78-534 (Washita River Watershed) and Public Law
83-566 Watershed Protection and Flood Prevention
Program. Forty-two of these dams are in Beckham
County.
The primary purpose of flood control dams is to reduce
flooding. The secondary benefits of the dams address a
myriad of public needs such as water supply, water
quality, soil health, water management, wetland
enhancement, fish and wildlife habitat, and recreation.
Flood control dams improve public safety, contribute to a
healthy economy and support a strong nation.
Watershed projects also include the installation of
natural resource conservation practices such as
terraces, waterways, ponds, gully repair, and pasture
and rangeland plantings. These conservation practices
improve water quality and soil health and reduce
sedimentation into the lakes formed by the dams.
Operation and Maintenance of Dams
The annual operation and maintenance of dams is the
responsibility of project sponsors (local units of
governments such as conservation districts).
Operation is the administrative and management
activities necessary to ensure the dams function as
designed and remain safe. Operation work includes
annual dam inspections and inspection immediately
following heavy rains.
Maintenance work includes removing trees from dams
and spillways, repairing erosion damage, repairing
damage to the spillway and dams after heavy
rainstorms, and keeping the principal spillway inlet
towers cleared of debris.
Operation and Maintenance Needs
Operation and maintenance of dams can be expensive
and labor intensive. $4 million is needed to operate and
maintain all 2,107 flood control each year. Only through
continued investment in operation and maintenance will
future generations enjoy the promise of safety these
dams offer.
section_path: Flood Control Dams in Beckham County

text:
Annual Benefits
The 2,107 flood control dams and conservation practices
in watershed projects provide $91 million in average
annual benefits. The table on the back of this page lists
the annual benefits provided by watershed projects in
Beckham County.

Rehabilitation and Dam Safety
As dams age some will need rehabilitation to remain
safe and protect the people that live or work
downstream.
At the conclusion of 2016, 260 flood control dams in the
state have been classified as high hazard. Of these 115
do not meet current state or federal safety criteria.
Approximately $300 million is needed to upgrade the
115 dams.
Of the 42 dams in Beckham County six are classified as
high hazard and have the potential for loss of life if they
should fail.
The number of high hazard dams will continue to
increase as long as residential and business
development is allowed downstream of the dam in the
breach flood area.
NRCS can provide 65 percent of the rehabilitation costs
and technical assistance to rehabilitate high hazard
dams. Local project sponsors provide 35 percent of the
cost and obtain any needed additional land rights.
As of December 2016 thirty-five dams in the state have
been rehabilitated and 18 others are in various stages of
planning, design or construction.

section: Adair Municipal Authority Receives $99,500 Grant for Wastewater System Improvements from the OWRB
text:
Tuesday, March 18, 2025

OKLAHOMA CITY — The Adair Municipal Authority (Authority) in Mayes County received approval for a $99,500 Rural Economic Action Plan (REAP) grant on Tuesday through the Oklahoma Water Resources Board (OWRB) to improve the Authority’s wastewater infrastructure.

The Adair Municipal Authority is undertaking a critical infrastructure project to replace a deteriorating sewer line that serves the local high school, including two newly constructed buildings. Originally installed in the 1960s, the 10-inch clay pipe is collapsing, posing risks to the wastewater system. The project will replace approximately 600 linear feet of 10-inch sewer line and 1,200 linear feet of 4-inch service line, along with all necessary appurtenances. The estimated cost of the project is $99,500, which will be fully funded by an OWRB REAP grant.

Lori Johnson, chief of the OWRB’s Financial Assistance Division, calculated that the grant will save the Authority’s customers $180,100 in principal and interest charges by not having to borrow the project funds.

“Ensuring the long-term functionality of critical infrastructure like wastewater systems is essential for the health and safety of our communities,” said Sen. Micheal Bergstrom, R-Adair. “This project will not only repair and enhance the infrastructure at Adair Public Schools but will help strengthen our local infrastructure overall. I appreciate all those at the Adair Municipal Authority and Oklahoma Water Resources Board for their efforts in securing this grant and for prioritizing the well-being of both our community and our schools.”

“Investing in our community’s infrastructure helps ensure long-term growth and sustainability,” said Rep. Rusty Cornwell, R-Vinita. “This funding will allow the Adair Municipal Authority to address much-needed wastewater system improvements, benefiting students and residents alike. I appreciate the Oklahoma Water Resources Board and Grand Gateway Economic Development Association for their continued support in strengthening our local communities.”

“We are grateful to Senator Micheal Bergstrom and Representative Rusty Cornwell for their continued support of water and wastewater infrastructure funding in Oklahoma,” said Julie Cunningham, OWRB Executive Director.

Since 1983, the Water Resources Board has approved over $7.8 billion in loans and grants for water and wastewater infrastructure improvements throughout Oklahoma.

Last Modified on Mar 19, 2025
section_path: Adair Municipal Authority Receives $99,500 Grant for Wastewater System Improvements from the OWRB

text:
The Circuit Engineering District #2 (CED2) is requesting $7,071,177 through the 2024 Bridge Investment Program (BIP) for the Adair: ICEE – Improving Connectivity and Enhancing Economic Well-being Project in Adair County, Oklahoma.

This project focuses on improvements to six bridges, enhancing connectivity at four key locations throughout rural Adair County. Throughout this document, the bridge locations will be referred to by the following names:

Bridges included in the project:
- Baron Fork Creek
- Bunch Road over Unnamed Creek
- England Hollow Creek (2 bridges)
- Little Lee Creek (2 bridges)

Each bridge will be reconstructed and widened to better handle increased traffic volumes and mitigate safety concerns. Additional improvements include the use of reflective paint, concrete traffic rail (TTR), and crashworthy guardrails to enhance safety and reduce collisions.

The Project addresses specific deficiencies at each bridge, allowing for a safer crossing and
infrastructure that meets current design standards. All six bridges will be replaced with new
structures designed with a 75-year anticipated lifespan. Comprehensive hydraulic analyses will
be conducted at all locations to ensure the bridges can withstand extreme weather conditions.
Without the improvements proposed with this project, all six bridges will require full closure
within four years, requiring excessively lengthy detours.

The Adair ICEE – Improving Connectivity and Enhancing Economic Well-being Project will address the goals of the Bridge Investment Program (BIP) by:

Project goals:
- Improving the safety, efficiency, and reliability of the movement of people and freight over each of the identified bridges.
- Upgrading the condition of six bridges, which reduces the number of bridges in poor or fair condition.
- Reducing the miles traveled over poor and fair condition bridges in the district and those bridges not meeting current design standards.
- Leveraging non-Federal contributions from the planning, design, and construction of eligible projects.

The ICEE project will advance four goals of the BIP program and improve the condition of six bridges that will otherwise require closure within the next four years.

All four sites are classified as low-income and areas of persistent poverty designated locations. Transportation to jobs, services, hospitals, and training are key to residents in the area, along with direct trucking access for the local agricultural and poultry industries.

Residents seeking employment, training, and medical facilities travel daily to and from Northwest Arkansas, Fort Smith, AR, and Tulsa, OK, the closest major metro areas. Their safety, travel time, and vehicle miles traveled are in jeopardy with deficient bridge infrastructure leading to closures at these four locations.

Currently, the Average Daily Traffic (ADT) range for each of the six bridges is 50 to 475. Heavier vehicles, such as trucks, school buses, and agricultural vehicles cannot use the bridges today, resulting in detours with increased vehicle miles traveled, travel time, increased emissions, and poor connectivity.

Enhanced network reliability is critical for sustainability of local agricultural industry operations, movement of emergency services throughout the county, and reliable, safe access to schools for local students.

section: Funding Request, Bridge Program Context, Planning, Local Investment, and Partnerships
text:
The total project cost for the six bridges is $14,142,353. This grant application requests a $7,071,177 investment (50% of total costs) from the Bridge Investment Program (BIP).

The $7.071M of BIP funding provides the necessary funding needed to improve quality of life for all that live, work, and travel through Adair County.

Locally, CED2 has invested in Adair County and understands the urgency of this project and continues to partner with local partners to move the project forward.

According to the benefit-cost analysis (BCA), this project achieves a benefit-cost ratio of 3.65, with a net present value of $32,799,279.

CED2 has had a large number of bridges that have required replacement in recent years due to poor condition and structural deficiencies as the system continues to age.

It has been a very challenging funding and programming challenge that the CED has not been able to navigate without closing several bridges across its many counties.

One of the top programming priorities going forward is avoiding as many bridge closures as possible by prioritizing bridges carrying the most traffic and/or with especially long detour routes, as well as those that are in the poorest condition and most likely to close in the short-term.

CED2 inspects its bridges in accordance with National Bridge Inspection Standards (NBIS), which requires inspection of all bridges at least once every 24 months. This enables CED2 to keep an updated list of bridges that are currently in critical condition or are anticipated to degrade to poor condition in the near term.

This approach to replacement is well-reflected in CED2’s annual Transportation Improvement Program.

CED2 held monthly district meetings beginning in 2021 to present, discussing the multiple bridges and needs in the region.

The bridges included in this application were identified at those meetings as priority projects based on connectivity for residents, structural condition, and projected life of bridges under four years.

CED2 staff then began environmental and design work for the sites.

CED2 is underway with final design for three of the four project components:
- England Hollow Creek
- Bunch Road over Unnamed Creek

Additionally, CED2 is currently conducting environmental work and preliminary design on Little Lee Creek.

To date, CED2 has incurred $1,855,814 in project development costs, including design, right-of-way, and utilities.

Funding for these activities came from local and state general fund transportation funds.

In addition, the Baron Fork Bridge is a critical regional need and currently in the Cherokee Nation’s Transportation Improvement Program.

The Cherokee Nation has also invested roadway funding in the Rock Fence and Bunch Roads, which include the Lee Creek and Bunch Bridges respectively.
section_path: Funding Request, Bridge Program Context, Planning, Local Investment, and Partnerships

- Baron Fork Creek

CED2 is the lead applicant for the Adair: ICEE – Improving Connectivity and Enhancing Economic Well-being Project.

CED2 regularly receives funds under Title 23, U.S.C. and has a successful history of partnering with agencies including local and county governments and tribal nations to complete projects.

As shown by the multiple letters of support, this project is a priority to multiple agencies to ensure critical rural bridges are replaced to continue the primary transportation corridors in the region.

Section: Additional Eligibility Requirements

The improvements for all project components will benefit pedestrians and bicyclists, who would be affected by bridge closures along with vehicular traffic.

Vehicular traffic volumes are low enough that pedestrian and bicycle travel can be accommodated on the proposed bridges with little potential for conflict.

ODOT is responsible for maintenance for on-system facilities throughout the state, including over 30,000 lane-miles.

ODOT has a $400M 4-Year Asset Preservation Plan which is both federally and state funded to address pavement and bridge condition throughout the state.

NBI data for each bridge are provided in the Appendix. The project encompasses four locations that require critical bridge improvements:

Bridge locations included in the project:
- Baron Fork Creek
- England Hollow Creek (2 bridges)
- Bunch Road over Unnamed Creek
- Little Lee Creek (2 bridges)

England Hollow Creek presents a unique location with two bridges. One is located on E761 Rd and the other is at NS 4695 Rd.

Little Lee Creek is a location consisting of two identified bridges listed in the NBI as well that will be replaced with a single bridge for safety and efficiency reasons.

The inclusion of four unique locations emphasizes the comprehensive scope of the project. The goal of this project is to enhance the safety, resilience, and efficiency of these critical bridges, contributing significantly to the well-being of the community.

section: Project Specific Information: Baron Fork Bridge & England Hollow Bridges
text:
Baron Fork Bridge

The Baron Fork Bridge is prioritized by CED2 Commissioner Sam Chandler due to the load posted for truck traffic weight limits — 19 tons.

The bridge is located on a major collector route, Piney Road, and connects State Hwy 51 north to US Hwy 62, along the Arkansas State Border.

The weight restriction for a legal crossing here is 19 tons, which requires all significant commercial traffic to re-route to US Hwy 59.

The detour route is 18.5 miles of additional road use due to the bridge restrictions, which increases greenhouse gas emissions due to the longer vehicle run-time.

The Baron Fork Bridge also has recurring maintenance issues, including roadway patches for the deteriorating deck and removal of river debris from against the support columns.

CED2 enters the river with equipment during the dry season once a year to remove the accumulated debris piles.

While in-place during other storm events, the debris pile causes a scour hole around the support pier, which threatens the stability of the structure.


England Hollow Bridges

England Hollow Road is an 8.5 mile major collector route, connecting US Hwy 59 north of Stilwell to the town of Christie.

The roadway alignment through the corridor at this location is an adverse factor for roadway safety. There is a history of cars leaving the roadway near this bridge project.

The goal is to align the roadway to improve the line of sight of the roadway at this intersection and replace the bridge in the correct location with proper drainage.

The intersection north of the England Hollow bridge has a limited line-of-sight, identified in the AASHTO guidelines.

Southbound traffic cannot see the vehicle in the northbound lane until after the vehicle is maneuvered across the center median strip.

Likewise, the westbound traffic that negotiate a left at this intersection to proceed southbound also have insufficient sight distance.

Historically, the roadway floods at this location. The two existing multi-cell bridge structures do not meet the current criteria for a major collector roadway conveyance.

Also, debris is a chronic nuisance at this location and amplifies the severity of the flooding.
section_path: Project Specific Information: Baron Fork Bridge & England Hollow Bridges

section: Project Specific Information: Bunch Road Bridge & Little Lee Bridges
text:
Bunch Road Bridge

The replacement of the Bunch Road bridge provides added safety, reliability and connectivity.

The load limit restriction of this bridge of 9 tons does not allow access for school bus traffic, which causes all residents and families with school age children to re-route.

This bridge services the east route around Bunch Mountain that accesses Starkiller Hollow, which parallels the railroad into the neighboring Sequoyah County.


Little Lee Bridges

The Little Lee Bridges are located on E 923 Rd, which is a 6.0-mile minor collector road between US Hwy 59 and Kirk Mountain Rd.

The road provides access to the communities of Bell and Short, Oklahoma.

A major flood scoured away both approach embankments on the concrete box bridge.

This deterioration necessitated the construction of the existing steel bridge, which re-established the route.

The concrete box bridges have critical scour, settlement, and deck delamination damage which resulted in a recommendation for closure by the bridge inspection engineer.

The bridge remains open today but is a risk to all travelers until it can be fully replaced.

The photos show the settlement and exposed rebar on the bridges.

Elected officials support the bridge remaining open to traffic, citing a full closure as an extreme hardship on local residents.

The load weight restriction of 8 tons does not allow for school buses or any substantial commercial traffic to use this route today.

The detour route for the bridges is approximately 20 additional miles to reach their destination on the other side.
section_path: Project Specific Information: Bunch Road Bridge & Little Lee Bridges

text:
CED2 requests $7,071,177 in BIP funding for construction of this project for the six bridges. This represents 50 percent of the total project costs.

All BIP funding will be used for eligible construction costs. Design and National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) costs will be funded through CED2 state funding allocation and other federal funding (formula funding).

NEPA will be completed prior to obligation of any BIP funds. Formula federal funding will fund the remaining future costs.

The requested BIP funding is entirely for construction costs. No other federal funds will be used for this project, and no other federal grants have been requested or awarded.

Total cost of the project, including all components, is estimated at $14,142,353. A complete breakdown of the costs and percentages is shown in Figure 3.

Project funding distribution:
- BIP Funds: $7,071,177 (50%)
- Non-Federal Funds (state and local): $3,535,587 (25%)
- Other Federal Funds: $2,469,648 (17%)
- Tribal Transportation Funds: $1,065,941 (8%)

Notes:
- Non-federal funds (state and local) will contribute $3,535,587.
- Other federal funds contribute $2,469,648.
- Cherokee Nation Tribal Transportation Funds contribute $1,065,941.

To date, CED2 and ODOT funded all pre-construction activities, including:
- NEPA
- Preliminary engineering
- Utilities relocation
- Right-of-way acquisition

These activities were completed to expedite the project for construction due to the urgency related to the life cycle of the bridges.

Construction cost estimates in Table 3 are based on current design stages for each bridge included in the project.

Cost estimate assumptions:
- Baron Fork Bridge, England Hollow bridges, and Bunch Road over Unnamed Creek are at 65 percent design.
- Both Little Lee Creek bridges are in preliminary design.

Estimates are based on:
- current engineer estimates
- quantities
- prices consistent with similar CED2 projects
- year-of-expenditure dollars

Future operations and maintenance costs will be covered 100 percent by state and local transportation funds.

A contingency of 15 percent is included in the estimate since the construction estimate is based on preliminary design. This accounts for inflation and future labor and supply chain cost increases.

The BIP requested funding is $7.071 million, which equals 50 percent of the total project cost and is well below the threshold allowed for BIP grants.

To date, CED2 has already expended $1,855,814 in project development costs.

Table 3 summarizes previously incurred expenses by CED2 across all project components.

BIP grant funding will greatly expedite construction of these critical bridges in Adair County. It will also help prevent CED2 from falling behind on future planned bridge replacement projects in eastern Oklahoma.

If BIP funds are not awarded, the timeline for project construction would extend to approximately 2030 or beyond.

Recent inflation in construction costs due to increases in materials and labor could push bridge projects out 10 years or more in the future, forcing CED2 to accomplish much less for the same investment.

Adair County travel patterns show increasing traffic volumes due to:
- increased truck traffic in eastern Oklahoma
- commuters traveling to the Tulsa metropolitan area from smaller rural communities

Rural residents and businesses depend heavily on these bridges.

If bridge closures occur due to deterioration:
- travel delays will increase
- congestion will worsen
- pollution will increase
- emergency access may be reduced

Without the safety enhancements proposed in this project, the quality of life for rural residents will decline.

The requested BIP funding will allow CED2 to address these transportation needs in a timely and efficient manner.

Grand Total Project Cost: $14,142,353

The Project includes four project components in Adair County in need of critical bridge
improvements. Among these, two include more than one structurally deficient and/or
functionally obsolete bridge: England Hollow Creek and the Little Lee Creek.
• England Hollow Creek is a unique site encompassing two separate bridges—one located on
E761 Rd and the other on NS 4695 Rd.
• The Little Lee Creek bridge replacement project includes a single bridge replacement of two
existing bridges, both listed in the NBI.
All six bridges proposed for BIP funding are currently in fair or poor condition. Out of the six
identified bridges:
• Four are structurally deficient (SD)
• Two are functionally obsolete (FO) and at risk of becoming structurally deficient.
All bridges will be replaced with new structures designed for a 75-year lifespan. Comprehensive
hydraulic analyses will be conducted at all locations to ensure the bridges can withstand
extreme weather conditions.
The six bridges slated for replacement were constructed between 1938 and 2002. Sufficiency
ratings vary from 25.8 to 79.30 out of 100. All six bridges have load restrictions and two are
rated for legal loads. This necessitates alternative routes for commercial trucks and school
buses. The horizontal clearances (i.e., deck width) of the bridges range from 18 to 24 feet for
two-lane bridges, all falling short of the desired roadway width for traffic needs. Five of the
six bridges handle 352 vehicles per day or less, while one carries approximately 475 vehicles
per day. Truck traffic constitutes roughly five percent of this volume, although certain bridges
currently carry significantly higher truck volumes, approaching 15 percent. Ratings of the six
bridges are provided in Table 4 on the next page.
Replacing the bridges in Adair County will reduce lifecycle costs. Should no major rehabilitation
or replacement occur, it is estimated the existing bridges will all require closure in the next
four years, as shown in Table 5. Maintenance costs decline significantly after closure, involving
only signage and barriers. Therefore, maintenance costs savings for the Project are small, but
positive ($3,788 per year), over the 20-year analysis period.

- ADT: 475

Little Lee Creek Bridges

- Anticipated Closure: Immediate

The existing conditions of the six bridges fall short in various critical aspects for safety.
Specifically, four of the six bridges do not meet current design standards, resulting in narrow
structures that cannot adequately accommodate two-way traffic or provide safe zones in the
event of breakdowns or accidents. Furthermore, none of the six bridges comply with modern
railing standards, ands lack railings altogether.
To address the shortcomings, specific enhancements will be used to improve safety.
This includes:
• Widening bridges to a 26-foot-wide deck to offer one 13-foot driving lane in each direction.
• The bridges will be revamped with two 12-foot lanes and 8-foot outside shoulders to better
handle increased traffic volumes. Reflective paint will be used for markings to ensure visibility
in adverse weather conditions, and all bridges will feature concrete traffic rail (TR3) to minimize
the potential for vehicles to leave the bridge.
The substandard condition of these bridges has far-reaching consequences for the communities
within Adair County. School bus routes have been adversely affected, with load restrictions and detours making travel unreliable and
potentially dangerous for students. Emergency
services have also faced challenges, as load
restrictions prevent vital emergency vehicles,
such as fire trucks, from crossing, potentially
leading to life-threatening situations.
Over the past decade, the six bridges have
witnessed four collisions and the severity of the
incidents has been unexpectedly high (Table 6).
Factors contributing to this include the narrow
width of the bridges, the absence of separation
between traffic directions, and the lack of
proper railings. Inattention and vehicles crossing
the centerline were frequently cited as causes,
along with fixed object collisions with guardrails
and culverts. The figure shows the crash severity
for all six bridges.
To mitigate these issues and enhance safety, the project includes widening all bridges and
installing crashworthy guardrails. According to the Federal Highway Administration (FHWA) Crash
Modification Factor clearinghouse, widening the bridges to 26 feet could potentially reduce
collisions by 24 to 75 percent, depending on the existing bridge width. Installing guardrails on
bridges is expected to reduce collisions up to 58 percent, while improving existing guardrails
could lower injury-related collisions by 22 percent.
These comprehensive improvements are crucial for the safety of those using the bridges and for
overall reliability of transportation and emergency services in the area.

text:
Improving the structural and geometric conditions of the six bridges enhances the mobility,
efficiency, and reliability of the movement of people and freight throughout Adair County and the
surrounding region. The 2023 total daily traffic counts for all bridges are 2,020 vehicles.
Rural Oklahoma is an economically productive part of the State and the nation. In 2020,
Oklahoma generated approximately $6.2 billion in agricultural cash receipts, roughly 3.3% of
the State Gross Domestic Product. Adair County is home to food processing, canning industries,
poultry farms and cattle ranches.
The six bridges are critical links in this rural area. Given their rural locations, closures of these
bridges would result in detours ranging from 1.5 to 20.2 miles. The savings in vehicle operating
costs and travel times from avoiding these detours are substantial. The roadway network reliability
is important in the agricultural industry and relies 100 percent on getting products to market cost-effectively. Unexpected delays due to bridge postings or closures add time and cost to transporting
materials and equipment, affecting carriers’ ability to provide on-time delivery, and impacting the
downstream supply chain.
This Project will directly lead to good-paying jobs in the construction sector. According to the
Bureau of Labor Statistics, Oklahoma’s highway, street, and bridge construction laborers make
a higher mean hourly wage than any other construction related jobs or industries. These wages
then create indirect economic benefits to the communities housing the workers. A $14 million
dollar construction contract for six bridges requires a large numbers of laborers to complete,
attracting employees and further enhancing economic benefits in northeastern Oklahoma.

The proposed bridge replacements within this rugged and rural county address climate change
through reduction of emissions and air pollution from motor vehicles by increasing connectivity
and shortening travel distances for residents and businesses. This increase in connectivity fosters
economic development in a rural community and supports disadvantaged and low-income
population access to employment, grocery and emergency services, schools, and other services.
Currently the bridges are either closed or load posted, requiring heavier vehicles such as
trucks, school buses, and agricultural vehicles to detour. Eliminating these closures and load
postings reduces the miles driven by trucks and decreases vehicle emissions and positively
impacts air quality. Detours also impact emergency response vehicles such as fire trucks, where
consequences of rerouting these vehicles could be a matter of life and death.
All census tracts in Adair County meet the Justice40 Initiative as outlined in Executive
Order 14008 – Tackling the Climate Crisis at Home and Abroad, created by the Council on
Environmental Quality (CEQ). The six bridges are within census tracts classified as both low
income (people in households where income is less than or equal to twice the federal poverty
level), historically disadvantaged areas, and areas of persistent poverty.
Median household income ranges from $23,064 to $42,702. Critical service gaps include
transportation access, food deserts, broadband internet, and a lack of health insurance. The
estimated drive time for basic services to grocery stores and medical facilities range from 4 to 16
minutes, and there are no points of interest (adult education, grocery stores, medical facilities,
and parks) within a 15-minute walk.
The proposed Project will improve the resiliency of functionally obsolete and at-risk infrastructure
by replacing the existing structures with ones designed to withstand future natural hazards. All
census tracts within the county have an expected population loss rate and economic loss to
agricultural value, resulting from natural hazards each year greater than the 90th percentile. All six bridges have an expected economic loss to building value rate from natural hazards greater
than the 90th percentile. Because all census tracts within Adair County are low income, included
in areas of persistent poverty, and historically disadvantaged, these losses from natural hazards
will disproportionately affect low income and disadvantaged populations.
Although the expected annual loss flooding risk overview for Adair County is relatively low when
compared to the rest of the US, (below the 90th percentile for projected flood risk to properties
from projected riverine floods and rain events within 30 years), social vulnerability to these losses
is very high and community resilience is very low (hazards.fema.gov/nri/map).
In addition to property damage, flooding can
also cut off access to utilities, emergency
services, transportation, and impact the overall
economic well-being of an area. Overall, Adair
County has a moderate risk of flooding over
the next 30 years, which means flooding is
likely to impact day-to-day life within the
community. The proposed Project will reduce
the vulnerability of the six bridges to increased
flooding and enhance the entire Adair County
transportation system capacity to cope with
extreme weather and future environmental
conditions, by providing alternative routes that
could be used to reach the same destination.

section: Criterion #5: Equity and Quality of Life
text:
This project contributes to the Equity,
Partnership, and Quality of Life criteria
connecting communities, and improving safety
for low income and disadvantaged populations.
As critical links for regional agricultural activity,
the six bridges provide improved mobility and
reliability for personal and business travel. In
this rural, sparsely populated, and low income
and disadvantaged part of the state, a reliable
transportation network able to carry large
overweight vehicles is critical to the livelihoods
of most residents. The project will improve
reliability and capacity and support quality of life for the region’s agricultural producers. These bridges will be safer and provide emergency
vehicles with better access.
Adair County is considered an Area of Persistent Poverty and a Historically Disadvantaged
Community as defined by the US Department of Transportation. Further, Adair County has the
highest percentage of Native Americans in its population than any other Oklahoma County. This
project will increase the quality of life for this underserved population by connecting them with
much needed services in the area.
section_path: Criterion #5: Equity and Quality of Life

text:
Many innovative approaches to project delivery can be utilized with the bundling of multiple
bridges. With six locations bundled into one project, single agreements can more easily be made
with public and private utility owners to relocate their lines in advance of project construction.
Adair Engineering District has established relationships with the local utility providers and will
coordinate any needed utility relocations. Another benefit of bundling is the economy of scale for
multiple aspects of the construction phases. CED2 intends to use a single contract award for this
project to save costs, as well as construction and procurement time. Bundling is anticipated to
save 10 percent over the costs of the individual bridges.
3D digital project plans will be required by CED2 for all four project components in order
to expedite project construction and minimize errors and issues encountered during the
construction process. The 3D digital plans will be provided to the contractor, including the
existing ground and proposed digital 3D surfaces. Contractors can utilize state-of-the-art GPScontrolled automated equipment throughout construction. This greatly reduces human error
when establishing grades and elevations and increases efficiency for the entire earthwork portion
of construction.

The primary objective of the project is to
enhance the condition of the bridges at
the four locations in Adair County, through
the replacement of six existing bridges. The
Benefit-Cost Analysis (BCA) considers the
costs associated with the project and the
benefits it will generate, with both qualitative
and quantitative assessments of the benefits
being considered.
The model for this analysis is in accordance
with the methodology outlined by the United
States Department of Transportation (USDOT)
in the Benefit-Cost Analysis Guidance for
Discretionary Grant Programs published in
December 2023. This analysis includes a 20-
year operational evaluation of each bridge.
All monetary values in the BCA, including
costs, are expressed in constant 2022 dollars.
The detailed data and calculations integral to
assessing the project’s benefits and costs are
outlined within the accompanying BCA memo. 
Based on the analysis, the project will generate
$45,191,395 in discounted benefits against
$12,392,116 in discounted capital costs, utilizing
a 3.1 percent real discount rate. This translates
into an anticipated Net Present Value (NPV) of
$32,799,279 of benefits, resulting in a Benefit/
Cost Ratio of 3.65. CED2 seeks $7,071,177 in
BIP funding to support this endeavor, totaling
$14,142,353 in construction costs.
Table 7 presents a compilation of all the
benefits evaluated for the project. The largest
proportion of the benefits, accounting for
approximately 82 percent, is attributed to travel
time savings and vehicle operating cost savings.
The reduction in collision costs accounts for
less than one percent of the overall benefits.
Maintenance cost savings account for 4.7
percent, while environmental cost savings
account for about 7.75 percent. Additionally,
the residual value of assets accounts for
approximately 5.9 percent of the total benefits.

Benefit Categories

Safety
- Benefit Amount: $214,336
- Share of Total Benefits: 0.47%

Travel Time
- Benefit Amount: $20,620,304
- Share of Total Benefits: 45.63%

Vehicle Operating Cost (VOC)
- Benefit Amount: $15,999,276
- Share of Total Benefits: 35.40%

Resilience
- Benefit Amount: $62,218
- Share of Total Benefits: 0.14%

CO2 Emissions
- Benefit Amount: $3,021,849
- Share of Total Benefits: 6.69%

Non-CO2 Emissions
- Benefit Amount: $471,301
- Share of Total Benefits: 1.04%

Other Environmental Benefits
- Benefit Amount: $20,472
- Share of Total Benefits: 0.05%

Maintenance Savings
- Benefit Amount: $2,120,417
- Share of Total Benefits: 4.69%

Residual Value
- Benefit Amount: $2,661,222
- Share of Total Benefits: 5.89%

Project Financial Summary

Total Benefits: $45,191,395  
Total Discounted Costs: $12,392,116  

Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR): 3.65  

Net Present Value (NPV): $32,799,279

Technical Feasibility and Technical Competency
This Project is subject to CED2 and ODOT design and construction specifications, specifically
preliminary plans and the NEPA process. Design criteria established for the project follow ODOT’s
Roadway Design Standards, Standard Specifications for Highway Construction and Drainage
Manual. Risks to scope, schedule, and budget are described in this application. As a recipient of
federal funds, CED2 is subject to Title VI of the Civil Rights Act, the Uniform Relocation Act, and
federal procurement requirements.
The project will replace six bridges at four locations within Adair County. CED2 has extensive
experience constructing bridges throughout Eastern Oklahoma, many of which have utilized
federal funds. They are familiar with federal requirements and have the technical capacity to
ensure compliance with applicable rules and regulations.
Project Schedule
Figure 6 shows the project schedule with major milestones, and CED2 is working towards
ensuring timely obligation of funds for the BIP Grant ahead of the USDOT statutory deadline. The
schedule shows the start and completion dates for design, environmental approvals, right-of-way
acquisition, utility relocations, and construction for the entire Project. Depending on construction
sequence, certain bridges would be completed and opened prior to the December 2028, as shown
in the figure.
Once the grant funds are obligated, construction for the Project will kick off nearly immediately.
• The NEPA process is completed for Baron Fork Creek and England Hollow Creek.
• CED2 is waiting for NEPA approval for Bunch Road and Little Lee Creek.
• The right of way (ROW) has been acquired for Baron Fork Creek and England Hollow Creek
and CED2 is in the process of acquiring two remaining locations, with the majority of ROW
acquisition taking place between 2023 and third quarter of 2026.
The permitting process for the Project is expected to be completed by 2024, with the U.S.
Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Clean Water Act (CWA) 404 permits for the final phase
(Phase 4) of the Project expected to directly follow. Permits for previous Project phases have
already been obtained, and no delays are anticipated. All state and local approvals have also been secured. Furthermore, Project letting is scheduled for the earliest location (Baron Fork Creek)
in September 2025, and construction is expected to begin the last quarter of 2025. Completion
of the last project is anticipated in fall 2028. All funds will be expended well in advance of the
statutory deadline of September 30, 2031.

Environmental Permits & Reviews
The environmental process is completed for Baron Fork Creek and England Hollow Creek.
This area does have several listed species of concern, including threatened, endangered, and
proposed bat species that can use bridge and box structures for roosting, and riparian corridors
for feeding. The Tribal nations also require coordination through ODOT’s Director of Tribal
Coordination. Environmental documentation is expected to be a Categorical Exclusion (CE)
a Programmatic CE (PCE) or an Individual CE (ICE). Programmatic CEs do not require FHWA
signature and are approved more quickly than ICEs. The NEPA process will be completed prior to
construction letting by CED2. All property owners will be notified, and owners and local school
districts, utilities and emergency service providers will be notified of any road closures in advance
of construction.
The independent cost estimates are complete for Baron Fork Creek. The England Hollow bridges
have the PCE in progress and are currently waiting on Cultural Resources Section 106 process
to be completed before moving forward. The South Bunch bridge and the Little Lee bridges will
begin the NEPA process in late 2025.
Other Permits and Approvals
• Environmental Permits: Environmental
Impact Statement (EIS) or Environmental
Assessment (EA).
• Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 404 Permit
from U.S. Army Corps of Engineers to
address impacts to water quality.
• Endangered Species Act (ESA) Consultation
if the review determines an endangered or
threatened species. CED2 will consult with
the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service or the
National Marine Fisheries Service.
• FHWA Approval for Design and Construction
for each site.
• Cultural Resource Clearances - Section
106 of the National Historic Preservation
Act: Consultation to identify and address
potential impacts on historic properties in
the study area.
Right of Way (ROW) Acquisition Plan
For each of the four project components, a comprehensive ROW acquisition plan is often a crucial
component. The ROW acquisition plan outlines the necessary land and property rights to be
obtained to facilitate the construction or improvement of a bridge. The plan will include a needs
assessment, property title data, appraisal data, public engagement, timelines, and costs. CED2 will
adhere to all relevant federal, state, and local regulations governing right of way acquisition.

section: Required Approvals: State and Local Approvals, Assessment of Project Risks and Mitigation Strategies
text:
State and Local Approvals
CED2 held monthly district meetings in 2021 through present, discussing the multiple bridges
and needs in the region. The four locations with six bridges were identified at those meetings
as priority projects based on recent structural inspection reports, and all four sites were added
to the Transportation Improvement Program. CED2 and consultant staff were directed to begin
environmental and design work at these sites. In addition, the Baron Fork Bridge is a high need
and currently on Cherokee Nation’s Transportation Improvement Program.
CED2 is underway with final design for three of the four project components - Baron Fork Creek,
England Hollow Creek, and Bunch Road over Unnamed Creek. Also, CED2 is currently conducting
environmental work and preliminary design on Little Lee Creek. To date, CED2 has incurred
$1,855,814 in project development costs, including design, right of way & utilities. Funding for
these activities came from local and state general fund transportation funds.
Total cost of the project is estimated at $14,142,353. ODOT is committing $3,535,587 in state
funding, a 25 percent match, and other federal funds will be used for the remaining $3,535,587.
The other federal funding includes Cherokee Nation through their Tribal Transportation Funds,
which is $1,065,941. The BIP funding request is $7.071M, which is 50 percent of the total costs
and well below the threshold allowed for rural grants. To date, CED2 expended $1,855,814 in
previously incurred costs.
Included in the Adair County BIP application are numerous letters of support from the
state agencies to local organizations to tribal nations. The Project has a high impact on the
transportation network in the region for enhanced connectivity and mobility. From the beginning
of the project and preliminary planning work, CED2 has provided consistent communication with
the local residents, elected officials, and major business in the area.
Assessment of Project Risks and Mitigation Strategies
Areas of project risk that these project components are most vulnerable to have included
environmental uncertainties, construction cost inflation, and potential procurement delays.
CED2 has initiated environmental review processes early for all four project components, having
completed two of them. The remaining two project components are anticipated to have their
environmental assessments complete by the end of 2025.
In order to mitigate construction cost inflation risk, contingency has been added to all
construction estimates and an allowance for inflation was included in the cost estimates to
achieve construction cost estimates that would be relevant for the year in which the projects are
to be let for construction.
Potential procurement delays remain a high-risk item, but CED2 has begun mitigating this risk by
ensuring the selection of standard bridge designs and more readily available structural elements
as much as possible across the four project components. In addition, ODOT and CED2 have built in delayed starts for certain projects in the past that were known to have design elements
requiring long lead times that were critical to completion of the project. In order to avoid high
mobilization (and remobilization) costs and eventual undesired contractor-requested contract
time delays to account for these material lead times, CED2 will consider building in a delayed
start, as needed, for project components that are considered highest risk for long material lead
times. The contractor will be selected for the contract and allowed to order materials, but they
will not be allowed to start work to avoid unnecessary exposure of the travelling public and
construction workers to work zone conditions and to avoid drawn-out construction durations.
section_path: Required Approvals: State and Local Approvals, Assessment of Project Risks and Mitigation Strategies

text:
CED2 understands the priorities of the administration and the goals outlined in
the departmental strategic plan, as demonstrated throughout this application.
CED2 focused this application on the key areas listed below.
• Safety and Infrastructure Resilience of the six bridges to be replaced.
• Sustainability and Environmental Stewardship of the designs with minimize disruption to
ecosystems or reduce the project’s carbon footprint.
• Innovation and Technology Integration used in the overall design of the bridges.
• The six bridges will be replaced with upgraded facilities accessible to all, following the
current bridge standards.
• CED2 will continue to involve the Adair County communities to ensure residents
understand the process, the project, and the realistic schedules for the project.
• The six replacement bridges will contribute positively to the economic development in
the region. CED2 ensures the six bridges projects will be in compliance with regulations
and follow the appropriate environmental process.
• CED2 will continue to foster collaboration with other government agencies, private
entities, and stakeholders to leverage resources for the project.
CED2 is partnering with ODOT for this initiative, leveraging BIP funds to bolster job opportunities
in Adair County. This strategic alliance aims to tap into Oklahoma’s 193 registered apprenticeship
programs, overseen by the US Department of Labor, which are pivotal to enhancing workforce
skills, particularly in key sectors like transportation. CED2 leverage the county’s workforce
development initiatives, particularly in utilizing existing apprenticeship programs and
contributing to local economic growth thorough job creation and infrastructure improvement.
To train and place workers from underrepresented populations, ODOT has launched a Small
Enterprise Training (SET) Program to facilitate increased participation in construction contracting opportunities by Oklahoma’s small businesses. SET will offer free training to Oklahoma-based
companies that meet National Small Business Administration (SBA) size guidelines and are
actively pursuing ODOT and CED contracting opportunities. SET’s goal is to assist certified
DBE’s, DBEs, and other small businesses currently involved in or interested in expanding their
operations, in the road construction industry. These workshops will help to increase the
participants’ business expertise and their capacity to acquire and perform contracts both in the
public and private sectors. The training program will include technical subjects such as bidding
and estimating costs in addition to business skills like networking and using social media as an
outreach tool.
In addition, as documented in the 2023 ODOT DBE Manual, ODOT has instituted equity-focused
policies related to project procurement and construction to ensure equity in the overall project
delivery and implementation. The mission of ODOT’s Contract Compliance Division (CCD) is to
ensure equal employment opportunity within ODOT, to level the playing field for DBE by providing
full and meaningful participation opportunities in ODOT’s federally funded highway projects and
to plan, implement and provide guidance to prevent discrimination in federal aid programs and
activities. ODOT CCD implements and oversees the ODOT DBE Program and the Unified Certification
Program for USDOT funded recipients, assuring compliance with 49 CFR Part 26. Both consultants
and construction contractors are required to meet the stated DBE commitments. ODOT CCD
conducts reviews of contractors and subcontractors at any time to ensure compliance.

As demonstrated with this grant application, one of the four project components has already been
closed to traffic, and the three remaining project components are expected to require closure to
through traffic within the next five years – by the end of 2028. The condition of the associated six
bridges has been poor and is nearly unsuitable for traffic based on structural inspection reports.
Without BIP funding, CED2 anticipates having to close all six bridges to traffic for an extended
period of time until adequate funding can be collected to address these critical locations. While
all six bridges are currently programmed for replacement, the schedule to replace them is not
expected to occur in time to avoid detours in excess of 20 miles in length. Said another way,
with the requested BIP funding, CED2 will be able to address not only these six bridge locations
as expeditiously as possible, but will be able to move up funding for various other at-risk bridges
across its 7-county footprint. Securing BIP funding as requested will make a real difference in
the ability of the District to stay ahead of the oncoming cascade of bridge replacements that it
understands are needed to continue to keep its county road network open and operating and to
keep moving people and cars safely across rural Eastern Oklahoma.
